title: Shipping industry warns IMO's net-zero delay could slow decarbonisation progress
description:
The framework includes a new fuel standard for ships and a global pricing mechanism for emissions, aiming for net-zero emissions by or around 2050.
https://www.wbcsd.org/news/wbcsd-statement-of-support-for-the-imo-net-zero-framework/ – The World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD) released a statement in September 2025 expressing support for the IMO’s Net-Zero Framework.
The industry highlighted the framework’s potential to incentivize the transition to net-zero emissions while ensuring a level playing field for companies engaged in international trade and logistics.
https://www.hilldickinson.com/insights/articles/imos-net-zero-framework-adoption-adjourned-and-consensus-building-continues – In October 2025, the IMO’s Marine Environment Protection Committee (MEPC) adjourned discussions on the Net-Zero Framework until 2026.
The article discusses the implications of this delay and the ongoing efforts to build consensus among IMO member states for the adoption of the Net-Zero Framework.
text:
Over 85 major players call on the International Maritime Organization to finalise its Net-Zero Framework in 2026, emphasising that further delays threaten crucial investments in green technologies and infrastructure for shipping’s transition to sustainability.

More than 85 major shipping-related companies have urged International Maritime Organization member states to finalise and adopt the IMO’s Net-Zero Framework in 2026, warning that further delay would undermine investment in alternative fuels, vessels and port infrastructure needed to decarbonise the sector. According to industry signatories, the framework, originally expected to be adopted in October 2025, was deferred to allow further consensus-building, with talks now scheduled to resume in 2026.

The open letter from shipowners, ports, fuel producers and technology providers framed the framework as a balanced compromise that can deliver a clear pathway to achieve net-zero shipping by or around 2050 and stressed that the international nature of IMO regulation creates a single rulebook that gives investors certainty. The signatories said that low-carbon fuels, new vessels and bunkering infrastructure are already being developed and that postponing adoption risks reversing progress.

The signatories reiterated language from the joint statement emphasising predictability for long-lived assets and warning that ‘confusion and uncertainty resulting from delay in the adoption of the framework risks undermining international investment and growth in the solutions needed to enable shipping’s energy transition’. They added that the approved framework provides a stronger signal for industry to scale up production and deployment of technologies.

The background to the appeal is the IMO’s own timeline: the organisation approved the Net-Zero Framework in April 2025 but the Marine Environment Protection Committee adjourned its formal adoption in October 2025 to allow member states more time to reach consensus and to develop implementation guidance. That intersessional work will continue into 2026, as the committee refines rules including a new fuel standard and a global carbon pricing mechanism intended to cover the bulk of emissions from large ocean-going ships.

Responses from civil society and industry groups have been mixed but generally supportive of a timely adoption. The World Business Council for Sustainable Development issued a statement backing the framework as essential to create a harmonised regulatory environment that enables business investment, while environmental advocates warned that the postponement delays urgently needed action and could push back implementation milestones originally linked to 2028.

Industry associations likewise pressed for a global approach to avoid fragmented national measures that would raise costs and complexity for international trade, underlining that a unified IMO framework is intended to preserve a level playing field while incentivising the transition to net-zero fuels and ships. With intersessional work under way in 2026 to prepare implementation guidelines, the signatories urged member states to conclude consensus quickly to sustain investor confidence and the momentum behind fleet and infrastructure investments.

name: Original Search
title: IMO Net-Zero Fund: Paving the Way to Decarbonized Shipping - Sea and Beyond
description:
The International Maritime Organization (IMO) is taking a historic step toward decarbonizing global shipping.
The IMO Net-Zero Fund (NZF), expected to mobilize $10–15 billion per year starting around 2028, is designed to help the sector achieve net-zero greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 2050.
Key Objectives of the Fund: Enable developing countries to transition to low-carbon fuels and technologies.
Adoption Process The fund requires adoption during an extraordinary MEPC session, with a two-thirds majority of Annex VI contracting parties.
Dependence on Funding: Developing countries may resist adoption without guaranteed access to the fund.
text:
The International Maritime Organization (IMO) is taking a historic step toward decarbonizing global shipping. The IMO Net-Zero Fund (NZF), expected to mobilize $10–15 billion per year starting around 2028, is designed to help the sector achieve net-zero greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 2050.

What is the IMO Net-Zero Fund?

The NZF framework, approved at MEPC 83 in April 2025, integrates fuel intensity targets, carbon pricing, and a dedicated funding mechanism to support the transition to low-carbon shipping technologies. Ships above 5,000 gross tonnage, responsible for 85% of sector emissions, are included under this framework.

A carbon price of $100 per tonne of CO₂e is proposed for these vessels, which will finance support for developing nations adopting green shipping technologies and infrastructure.

Key Objectives of the Fund:

Enable developing countries to transition to low-carbon fuels and technologies.

Encourage research and development in green fuels, propulsion systems, and energy-efficient shipping technologies.

Support a just transition for countries managing economic and workforce impacts.

Adoption Process

The fund requires adoption during an extraordinary MEPC session, with a two-thirds majority of Annex VI contracting parties. Preliminary voting in April 2025 indicated:

63 in favor

16 against

24 abstentions

After formal adoption, a tacit acceptance period of 10+ months allows objections. Any objections must meet one of two conditions to block implementation:

≥1/3 of participating countries

Representing >50% of global shipping tonnage

Challenges to Adoption

While the NZF is a critical step toward decarbonization, several impediments exist:

Economic Concerns: Higher operational costs, especially for developing nations.

Technological Limitations: Low-carbon fuels and retrofitting solutions are not yet fully scalable.

Geopolitical Tensions: Opposition from the U.S. and some Middle Eastern nations citing economic disadvantages.

Dependence on Funding: Developing countries may resist adoption without guaranteed access to the fund.

Global Positions

Supporters: EU, China, Brazil, and several developing nations anticipating fund access.

Opponents: U.S., Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, and some developing countries lacking technological capacity.

India’s Position:

India supports the NZF but emphasizes transparent and structured allocation:

50% for green fuel adoption

30% for R&D

20% for just transition policies

India is also implementing domestic initiatives, including green ports, vessels, and hydrogen hubs, complementing global decarbonization efforts.

Commercial and Strategic Implications

The NZF is not just an environmental tool; it carries commercial and strategic interests:

Commercial Gains: Benefits green fuel suppliers, shipbuilders, ports, and carbon market participants.

Strategic Gains: Donor countries may influence technology adoption, strengthen global leadership, and promote climate objectives.

How the Fund Will Work

Funding Source: Primarily shipping companies operating vessels >5,000 GT.

Collection Mechanism: Contributions may be collected by flag states and remitted to the IMO.

Voluntary Contributions: Developed countries can supplement the fund to assist developing nations.

Projected Revenue: Fully applied, the fund could generate up to $85 billion annually.

Beneficiaries:

Developing countries (low-carbon infrastructure, technology adoption)

R&D projects (30% allocation for green shipping technologies)

Just transition support (20% allocation)

Commercial suppliers and ports investing in green infrastructure

Why It Matters

The IMO Net-Zero Fund represents a landmark effort to decarbonize one of the most critical global industries. By linking carbon pricing, innovation, and targeted financial support, it not only drives environmental change but also opens new avenues for green technology, global partnerships, and sustainable shipping.

name: International Maritime Organization
title: EU Commission Unveils New Ports and Maritime Industry Strategies for Energy Transition
description:
EU Commission Unveils New Ports and Maritime Industry Strategies for Energy Transition The European Commission has presented new strategies for European Union ports and the industrial maritime sector, according to a statement from Hydrogen Europe.
The Ports Strategy positions ports as critical infrastructure, describing them not only as transport nodes but also as industrial and energy hubs.
The strategy outlines a role for ports as multi-fuel facilities to facilitate the rollout of sustainable maritime fuels.
The separate Industrial Maritime Strategy aims to strengthen the EU's maritime manufacturing sector and its related value chain.
This initiative responds to a perceived loss of competitiveness for the European maritime industrial sector on a global scale.
text:
EU Commission Unveils New Ports and Maritime Industry Strategies for Energy Transition

The European Commission has presented new strategies for European Union ports and the industrial maritime sector, according to a statement from Hydrogen Europe. The strategies outline a vision for the future of these sectors, recognizing their role in the bloc's energy transition, resilience, and competitiveness.

The Ports Strategy positions ports as critical infrastructure, describing them not only as transport nodes but also as industrial and energy hubs. This positioning is seen as central to deploying clean fuels, ensuring supply chain security, and supporting geopolitical positioning. The strategy outlines a role for ports as multi-fuel facilities to facilitate the rollout of sustainable maritime fuels. It also indicates Commission support for investments in clean fuel infrastructure and renewable fuels in countries outside the EU. Furthermore, the strategy seeks partnerships for energy cooperation in and around port areas, including for the use of hydrogen.

The separate Industrial Maritime Strategy aims to strengthen the EU's maritime manufacturing sector and its related value chain. This initiative responds to a perceived loss of competitiveness for the European maritime industrial sector on a global scale. The strategy identifies a need to close the cost gap between conventional and alternative fuels, citing the EU Emissions Trading System and the FuelEU Maritime Regulation as tools for this purpose. The Commission is strongly encouraging member states to allocate a portion of their revenues from the emissions trading system back into maritime decarbonization investments. A potential EU mechanism under a forthcoming revision of the emissions trading system is also being considered to support the uptake of sustainable fuels.

Additional efforts include the Commission pursuing engagement with the International Maritime Organization and forming strategic partnerships with third countries and market players. This engagement is envisioned through a European network of green shipping lanes and hubs.

The Commission has decided not to include a minimum supply obligation on fuel suppliers for sustainable maritime fuels within the Ports Strategy, a move seen as backtracking on earlier ambition. Instead, the Commission will look to the Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation, under which it will consider measures to accelerate the deployment of infrastructure for these fuels. While acknowledging the need for certainty around alternative fuel infrastructure, Hydrogen Europe recommends further ensuring clean fuel offtake by strengthening binding targets for renewable fuels of non-biological origin under the FuelEU Maritime regulation. The organization also states that the EU must lead in raising ambition at the International Maritime Organization while avoiding double legislation and not lowering its own domestic ambitions.

name: Shipping Industry
title: The UGS welcomes the European Industrial Maritime Strategy and calls for shipping at ... - Euractiv
description:
The Union of Greek Shipowners (UGS) has welcomed the EU Industrial Maritime Strategy, highlighting its recognition of the strategic importance of shipping for Europe’s economy, security and global competitiveness.
In this context, the UGS considers the European Industrial Maritime Strategy an important step toward ensuring consistency between EU strategic objectives and its policy choices.
Regional punitive measures for an international strategic sector only harm the EU shipping’s global competitiveness.
The UGS also stressed the importance of facilitating access to competitive ship finance to ensure the sustainability of EU shipping companies.
The EU Industrial Maritime Strategy comes at a crucial time for the EU and rightly recognises this reality”, she said.
text:
The Union of Greek Shipowners (UGS) has welcomed the EU Industrial Maritime Strategy, highlighting its recognition of the strategic importance of shipping for Europe’s economy, security and global competitiveness.

According to UGS President Melina Travlos, “the Strategy comes at a critical moment for the EU”, as the global geopolitical and economic landscape continues to shift rapidly. The Union of Greek Shipowners stresses that Europe must respond to these developments with clarity and strategic purpose.

Citing the findings of the Draghi Report on the Future of European Competitiveness, the UGS underlined that Europe’s economic strength and resilience are closely linked to its maritime sector. Around 90% of global trade is transported by sea, while nearly 75% of the EU’s extra-EU trade is carried by ships. At the same time, approximately three-quarters of the EU-controlled fleet operates in bulk/tramp shipping, securing the transport of essential commodities such as energy and food.

In this context, the UGS considers the European Industrial Maritime Strategy an important step toward ensuring consistency between EU strategic objectives and its policy choices.

The Greek shipowners’ representative body notes that the strategy sends a positive signal in pursuing the enhancement of the international competitiveness of European shipping, which is vital for the industry facing fierce international competition.

The UGS also welcomed the strategy’s recognition that access to clean maritime fuels are necessary for shipping’s decarbonisation, a particularly hard to abate sector.

At the same time, the UGS pointed to the importance of reinvesting revenues generated from shipping’s contribution to decarbonisation, including under the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) and FuelEU Maritime, as long as they apply, into supporting the sector’s energy transition. Targeted financial instruments will be necessary to bridge the price gap between conventional and new alternative fuels and to ensure that fuel suppliers provide safe and regulation-compliant fuels in sufficient quantities across Europe. In addition, however, introducing binding obligations for EU fuel suppliers to ensure fuel availability remains essential.

The strategy must remain consistent with international developments. Given the global nature of shipping, maintaining a level playing field at international level remains essential. Regional punitive measures for an international strategic sector only harm the EU shipping’s global competitiveness. At the same time, the sector’s transition must remain achievable and technology-neutral, allowing for pragmatic solutions, including transitional fuels such as LNG and biofuels, towards decarbonisation.

Safety of crews and vessels must always be the overarching principle. It is most important that the much-needed upskilling and reskilling of seafarers is among the high priorities included in the strategy for the competitiveness of the sector in line with international law and regulations.

The UGS also stressed the importance of facilitating access to competitive ship finance to ensure the sustainability of EU shipping companies. In addition, targeted measures, such as removing undue restrictions in the EU Taxonomy could significantly support the sector’s transition. Reducing unnecessary administrative and financial burdens, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises, will also be critical for the effective implementation of the strategy.

Commenting on the strategy, UGS President Melina Travlos said that the initiative reflects the strategic role of maritime transport for Europe’s economy and security.

“Shipping is not merely a business sector. It is a strategic pillar of the European economy and a guarantor of energy and food security. The EU Industrial Maritime Strategy comes at a crucial time for the EU and rightly recognises this reality”, she said.

Travlos also emphasised that shipping must lie at the heart of Europe’ s strategic policy making, as the latter seeks to strengthen its strategic autonomy and global influence.

“Europe’s competitiveness and security passes through shipping. Safeguarding the EU’s maritime leadership while enabling a pragmatic energy transition of its shipping industry is a sine qua non for the continent’s resilience and long-term prosperity.”

name: Biofuels & Shipping
title: Skuld's experiences on biofuels
description:
Biofuels are renewable fuels derived from biological sources, such as agricultural crops, waste, or residues, designed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
The first type of biofuels we received questions on was FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters).
Another type of biofuels is CNSL (Cashew Nutshell Liquid) which is an example of the explored options for ‘drop-in fuels.
These issues were detected by extended Gas Chromatography Mass Spectrometry (GCMS) testing of Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) and Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) samples from the vessels.
Biofuel as fuel oil ‘drop in fuel’ is already in the market.
text:
Biofuels are renewable fuels derived from biological sources, such as agricultural crops, waste, or residues, designed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

Since we received our first inquiry on the use of biofuels back in the last quarter of 2019, we have seen different kinds of challenges.

The first type of biofuels we received questions on was FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters). FAME is a mixture of Fatty Acid Methyl Ester that has similar properties to those of conventional fuel, and this type of biofuel is a good alternative as a ‘drop-in-fuel’.

However, FAME is unstable due to either unsaturated products in the fuel or the oxygen in the fuel, causing rancidification over time. FAME-based fuels can increase their instability if alcohol or water is present, and they are susceptible to thermal degradation and degradation if the fuel is exposed to light. It also has poor long-term stability and poor cold-flow properties. When FAME oxidises, the fuel becomes increasingly acidic and viscous, forming solid deposits that can cause operational issues for the engine, such as blocking filters and pipes, leading to fuel starvation and engine problems.

So far, from various trials and consumption on board insured vessels, our experience is that FAME is reliable and effective. FAME-based biofuels are reliable ‘drop-in fuels’ that can be readily used with existing ship engines, but of course, with the approval of the engine manufacturer.

Another type of biofuels is CNSL (Cashew Nutshell Liquid) which is an example of the explored options for ‘drop-in fuels. CNSL is a byproduct of cashew nuts and serves as a low-cost, renewable fuel alternative. Its characteristics include high levels of acidity, which make it highly corrosive and have poor combustion properties. However, it does improve lubricity and the energy content of the fuel blend. CNSL typically contains phenolic compounds, which have a high chance of polymerisation and can become unstable.

Recently, during the last quarter and first quarter of 2024/2025, Skuld has seen several vessels reporting operational problems, mainly related to the fuel system, like injector failure, filter and heater clogging, fuel sludging, and deposit build-up due to fuel contamination. These issues were detected by extended Gas Chromatography Mass Spectrometry (GCMS) testing of Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) and Very Low Sulphur Fuel Oil (VLSFO) samples from the vessels. The tests showed a high presence of phenolic compounds of cardol, cardanol and anacardic acid.

CNSL blends can cause serious problems for engine components, including accelerated wear and tear on the fuel pump, cracks in the fuel system, and poor engine performance, resulting in a loss of power.

Lately we received some inquiries from our members on UCOME (Used Cooking Oil Methyl Ester) as this is cheaper alternative. This is derived from the waste-based oil, cooking oil and animal fats, typically blended with conventional fuels such as B24 or B30 blends. UCOME is fully compatible with the existing marine engines as a drop-in fuel.

However, some challenges we have seen with this type of biofuel are limited supply and operational stability. UCOME has tendency to absorb water, potentially leading to microbiological growth and filter clogging. There is also the issue of long-term storage like any other type of biofuels. At the time of writing we have not seen any claims from our members on damage to engines related to UCOME blended fuels.

With stringent decarbonisation regulations already in force within the EU/EEA, the European authorities are now focusing on greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from ships sailing in EU/EEA waters and on the GHG intensity of the fuels burnt on board. The shipping industry needs to shift to low- or zero-carbon-emission fuels, including various biofuels.

Skuld’s key takeaways from our members' experience.

Biofuel as fuel oil ‘drop in fuel’ is already in the market.

Fuel quality is important for the main engine and auxiliary engines.

ISO standard ISO 8217:2024 for Bio residual marine fuel specification is advised to be used when testing the fuel.

A fuel analysis should always be carried out before using the bunkered fuel.

Not to use untested or unestablished biofuel products for marine diesel engines.

It is important to always conduct a good vetting procedure when selecting bunker suppliers.

Members may contact Skuld’s Loss Prevention department for any clarification on this topic.

name: Original Search
title: Will shipping's bet on onboard carbon capture pay off? - Riviera
description:
Over the last year, Solvang’s ethylene carrier Clipper Eris has served as a floating laboratory, with the goal of helping shipping turn the corner on onboard carbon capture and storage (OCCS).
Meantime there’s a ferment of activity in onboard carbon capture.
Seabound says the technology is 10 times cheaper than other carbon capture methods.
For Seabound chief executive and co-founder, Alisha Fredriksson, simplicity and compactness are the secret: “We realised that to make carbon capture work on ships, we only had to capture the CO2 onboard.
Although this isn’t an OCCS operation, it’s clear that onshore storage will play a big part in onboard carbon capture.
text:
Over the last year, Solvang’s ethylene carrier Clipper Eris has served as a floating laboratory, with the goal of helping shipping turn the corner on onboard carbon capture and storage (OCCS).

Bristling with technology, such as Wärtsilä’s carbon capture system, exhaust cleaning and onboard CO2 storage tanks, the target since the re-equipped vessel was launched in early 2025 was to slash emissions by between 70-80%.

Solvang chief executive, Edvin Endresen, is a believer. “This stands out as one of the more promising solutions for future vessels”, he declared last year.

And in anticipation of Clipper Eris proving him right, Mr Endressen has commissioned another seven Solvang OCCS-installed Panamax-size newbuilds from HD Hyundai Heavy Industries (HD HHI). Due for delivery during 2026 and 2027, these 88,000-m3 very large gas carriers will attract considerable interest amid a widespread uncertainty over future fuels.

Meantime there’s a ferment of activity in onboard carbon capture.

British start-up Seabound is taking the different route of calcium looping – a retrofit decarbonisation system that captures CO2 directly from a ship’s exhaust. Seabound says the technology is 10 times cheaper than other carbon capture methods. Installed near the funnel, it harnesses fumes that react with lime (calcium oxide) pebbles to form calcium carbonate (limestone). Stored in containers, the pebbles are offloaded onshore. Seabound says calcium looping captures up to 95% of CO2 and up to 98% of sulphur.

“This stands out as one of the more promising solutions for future vessels”

After pilot projects, Seabound completed its first full-scale commercial job in early 2026 on a cement carrier, the 5,700-gt UBC Cork managed by InterMaritime and chartered by Heidelberg Cement. Importantly for cost, the system is compatible with HFO.

For Seabound chief executive and co-founder, Alisha Fredriksson, simplicity and compactness are the secret: “We realised that to make carbon capture work on ships, we only had to capture the CO2 onboard. We could save regeneration and liquefaction for land. In doing so, we could minimise equipment onboard to reduce capital expenditure and [avoid] prohibitive space requirements.”

Seabound is also working with energy giants on regeneration facilities near key ports that would service ships unloading captured carbon – but that’s for the future.

What’s wrong with HSFO?

Another technology designed to appeal to cost-conscious ship operators is Value Maritime’s Filtree, a prefabricated plug-and-play system that also promises to capture carbon from HFO-fuelled engines. The carbon is stored in onboard tanks – “CO2 batteries” – and repurposed elsewhere, for instance in agriculture that “supports the circular economy”, explains Value Maritime.

Filtree is heading for bigger things. Several coastal vessels, starting with Visser Shipping’s Nordica in 2021, are already running with the system. But in early-2025 Value Maritime installed in Singapore its exhaust-cleaner and carbon-capture technology on Mitsui OSK’s long-range product tanker, the 75,000-dwt Nexus Victoria, its biggest installation to date. This is Value Maritime’s next-generation system that can be scaled up to capture 30% of CO2 emissions. Looking further ahead, Value Maritime is pursuing a strategy of end-to-end carbon capture solutions by integrating with onshore storage and, ideally, utilisation, for instance into the production of synthetic fuel.

“Emissions during land transport were substantial”

Mitsui OSK (MOL) is on a mission with carbon capture under its company-wide Environmental Vision 2.2 roadmap. “This system represents a crucial step in decarbonising vessels that cannot yet transition to next-generation fuels,” said Hiroyoshi Kubo, executive officer of MOL’s tanker unit about Filtree. “Together with Value Maritime we are committed to advancing carbon capture solutions and building a CO2 value chain that contributes to a sustainable, carbon-neutral industry.”

MOL is also plunging into the CO2 tanker business. In January the Japanese group signed long-term charter deals with the fledgling Northern Lights project for two new liquefied CO2 carriers. Said to be the world’s first cross-border CO2 transport and storage business, Northern Lights is a partnership between Equinor, Shell and TotalEnergies. Although this isn’t an OCCS operation, it’s clear that onshore storage will play a big part in onboard carbon capture. The 12,000-m3, ice-class newbuilds are due for delivery from HD HHI in 2028, sailing between Europe and a receiving terminal in Norway.

MOL’s new tankers will be busy. Launched in 2025, Northern Lights expects to ship and store 1.5M tonnes of CO2 a year, rising to 5m by 2028. The CO2 ends up in permanent storage 2,600 m under the seabed of the North Sea via a 100-km-long pipeline.

CO2 tankers

The pioneering CO2 carrier fleet is expanding. Hyundai Mipo is building four dual-fuel, 22,000-m3 capacity tankers for Capital Gas Ship Management that will be fitted with another carbon-stripping technology known as Erma First Carbon Fit. In this process a technology known as amine absorption sucks CO2 from flue gases. The mix is then heated, which separates the CO2 from the solvent. Finally, the CO2 is liquefied by Babcock LGE’s ecoCO2 system and stored onboard in low-temperature containers for offloading.

But offloading where? Although various discussions are going on with major ports such as Rotterdam, the race is on to establish enough storage capacity onshore. There could be too much captured carbon and too many CO2 tankers unless the situation is redressed rapidly, for instance the relative absence of discharge infrastructure.

Yet there’s hope. In the kind of co-operation that observers say is more typical of Asia than Europe, Japanese oil company ENEOS, NYK Line and Knutsen are exploring floating CO2 storage that could, according to NYK executive officer, Tsutomu Yokoyama, “be a game changer, significantly contributing to the reduction of costs and land area required for the liquefaction and storage process, which is a challenge for the commercialisation of CCUS.” ClassNK has already given approval in principle for the design of the FLSU.

OCCS fitted on container ship

Meantime there’s some heavyweight studies going on. Singapore’s Global Centre for Maritime Decarbonisation’s Project Captured research with Ever Top, a 14,110-TEU Neopanamax, the world’s first container ship to be retrofitted with a full OCCS system, is yielding interesting results. In mid-2025, GCMD successfully demonstrated the world’s first ship-to-ship offloading of onboard captured and liquefied CO2, coming up with some valuable lessons.

“We are already seeing huge interest in the market for this solution”

First, this pilot project was able to achieve nearly 8% of savings on a gross capture rate of 10.7%. However it also revealed important inefficiencies that limited savings of GHG emissions, such as the absence of a waste heat recovery system on the vessel that incurred a “fuel penalty” of 5%. If Ever Top had a waste heat system, the penalty would have dropped to 1.5%.

Also because the carbon was offloaded to a truck and transported 2,200 km, emissions during land transport were substantial. However the study concludes that improvements in the process could boost savings to nearly 18%, if the carbon is used rather than permanently stored. And under different scenarios a gross capture rate of up to 40% would be possible and emissions savings would rise as high as 68-71% if the saved carbon was utilised.

OCCS: the way forward?

Meantime there’s Clipper Eris and, on the basis of results from the vessel, Wärtsilä is confident that carbon capture is the way forward – for now: “The following types of vessels will find it particularly interesting: tankers; LNG carriers; offshore vessels; bulkers; and merchant vessels. Before alternative fuels become available at scale, installing a carbon capture system can ensure compliance with mid-term IMO targets while still sailing on fuels that are widely available.”

Wärtsilä believes the pilot project has proven the technical viability of its carbon capture solution and, although the group did not share details, president and chief executive, Håkan Agnevall, is impressed by the reaction: “We are already seeing huge interest in the market for this solution. Ahead of shipping’s net-zero targets, this new technology complements the industry’s ongoing efforts to dramatically reduce emissions from vessels and prevent stranded assets.”

The lessons from Clipper Eris are being incorporated into Solvang’s seven other CCS-ready vessels that will also run on HFO. However, Wärtsilä points out that the technology can be applied to the exhaust from any carbon-based fuel – such as HFO, methanol, LNG and MGO. Concluded Mr Endressen: “CCS provides a significant shortcut for achieving meaningful sustainability.”

And the price could be right. After six years developing its CCS technology, during which it captured 10 tonnes of CO2 a day from one of its marine engines at a research and test centre in Norway, Wärtsilä is confident of its numbers. Inclusive of capital and operating costs, its CCS technology would have a carbon-capture price of €50-€70/metric tonne of CO2 [US$54-$76].

As all operators point out though, there are high hurdles to clear: extra space required for storage; extra fuel required for the capture process (up by 70-100%); and the aforementioned need for offloading infrastructure.

name: Biofuels & Shipping
title: ICCT: Climate and revenue considerations for the IMO Net-Zero Framework - SAFETY4SEA
description:
Last April, the IMO approved the Net-Zero Framework to achieve its goal of net-zero emissions by or around 2050.
The International Council on Clean Transportation (ICCT) brief explains how IMO could address these elements to reduce real-world greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and promote climate justice.
Ships may use drop-in biofuels such as hydroprocessed vegetable oils, also referred to as renewable diesel (RD).
These biofuels can be produced using feedstocks such as waste oils and fats or virgin vegetable oils, including soy or palm.
However, because production requires significant upfront investment, the levelized cost of advanced fuels is not competitive with hydroprocessed vegetable oil within the IMO framework.
text:
Last April, the IMO approved the Net-Zero Framework to achieve its goal of net-zero emissions by or around 2050. While the vote to formally adopt the framework was delayed for at least a year, a research brief by the International Council on Clean Transportation (ICCT) has focused on two key elements of the framework: the use of biofuels for compliance and the funding for shipping-related climate mitigation.

The International Council on Clean Transportation (ICCT) brief explains how IMO could address these elements to reduce real-world greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and promote climate justice.

Without proper safeguards on these elements, emissions could actually increase, and revenues would not be enough to support climate change mitigation and adaptation in the shipping sector.

The analysis was conducted within the IMO NZF’s structure as agreed in April 2025, though the final policy may differ. Nevertheless, the findings are expected to be relevant for any IMO policy that aims to regulate the GHG intensity of marine fuels and to put a price on GHG emissions.

Regarding biofuels, food- and feed-based biofuels can be used to comply with the Net-Zero Framework or similar policies because they are commercially available and relatively inexpensive compared with advanced fuels such as e-fuels or cellulosic biofuels.

However, these “first generation” biofuels are associated with high indirect land-use change (ILUC) emissions that could undermine their potential emission benefits and, in some cases, could lead to a net increase in emissions.

Blending biofuels made from virgin vegetable or waste oils could be cost-effective relative to the Base target compliance cost of $380/t CO2e, but remain more expensive than the Tier 1 penalty of $100/t CO2e.

Furthermore, the analysis assumes that shipowners would minimize compliance costs by meeting the Base targets to avoid the Tier 2 penalty and paying only the Tier 1 penalty. It finds that projected annual revenues of $11–$12 billion generated under the current Net-Zero Framework are unlikely to cover the cost of an energy transition in shipping, which is estimated to be as much as $95 billion per year.

Blending crop-based biofuels for compliance

The current Net-Zero Framework is technology-neutral and does not exclude any fuel type; instead, fuels generate credits or deficits solely based on their direct supply chain life-cycle emissions. This means ships will likely use fuels that offer the greatest direct GHG savings per dollar for compliance.

One of the most cost-effective ways for shipowners to reduce GFI in the near term is blending biofuels. Ships may use drop-in biofuels such as hydroprocessed vegetable oils, also referred to as renewable diesel (RD).

These biofuels can be produced using feedstocks such as waste oils and fats or virgin vegetable oils, including soy or palm. Blending with these types of biofuels is an attractive option because they are commercially available and relatively inexpensive compared with emerging advanced fuels such as e-fuels and cellulosic biofuels.

These fuels are also compatible with existing engine and fueling infrastructure and, depending on the fuel and the life-cycle assessment (LCA) approach used, could have lower GFIs than conventional fuels. For example, advanced fuels made from cellulosic wastes and residues could theoretically be produced at larger capacities without feedstock constraints, providing deeper decarbonization.

However, because production requires significant upfront investment, the levelized cost of advanced fuels is not competitive with hydroprocessed vegetable oil within the IMO framework.

The GHG abatement costs of blending biofuels made from virgin vegetable or waste oils could be cost-effective relative to the Base target compliance cost of $380/t CO2e.

However, waste oils and fats are scarce and increasingly used in other sectors for fuel production, including those with a higher willingness to pay than shipping, such as road transport and aviation.

According to previous ICCT analysis, if all available waste cooking oil used for biofuel production today were diverted to the shipping sector, the fuel produced would at most cover around 5% of the annual energy demand of ships subject to the Net-Zero Framework.

Further, the framework could create demand for vegetable oil that is 3.5 times the amount currently used in the transport sector (40 billion liters in 2024), reaching a projected 139 billion liters by 2035.

title: Burando Energies debuts in Zeebrugge, adds new barge for biofuel and methanol supply
description:
Built in 2019, MTS ODYSSEE offers a total cargo capacity of 4,050 m³ and immediately enhances Burando Energies’ supply capabilities.
Additionally, MTS ODYSSEE is fully equipped to supply methanol, an alternative marine fuel gaining strong momentum and for which Burando Energies has already received multiple customer requests.
It also signified that Shandong Port Group has officially established a complete green methanol supply chain from manufacturing to transportation and storage and then port bunkering, becoming the first port in Northern China to achieve a full-chain green methanol bunkering service for ships.
Launched last year, the Pilbara Clean Fuel Bunkering Hub outlines a strategic approach to establish a clean fuel bunkering hub in the region, enabling the use of alternative fuels such as ammonia.
Related: Pilbara Ports releases roadmap to establish Australia’s first clean fuel bunkering hub Photo credit: Pilbara Ports Published: 6 March, 2026
text:
European marine fuel supplier Burando Energies on Thursday (5 March) said it has expanded its fleet and entered Zeebrugge market with sustainable marine fuels including Green Methanol.

The company welcomed the estuary barge MTS ODYSSEE to its fleet under a time charter agreement, a strategic move that marks a significant expansion of our operational footprint in North-West Europe.

Built in 2019, MTS ODYSSEE offers a total cargo capacity of 4,050 m³ and immediately enhances Burando Energies’ supply capabilities.

“By entering the Port of Zeebrugge, we position ourselves closer to clients in a key maritime hub where demand for reliable and alternative fuel solutions continues to grow,” the company said in a social media post.

The vessel will primarily be deployed for the delivery of biofuels. Additionally, MTS ODYSSEE is fully equipped to supply methanol, an alternative marine fuel gaining strong momentum and for which Burando Energies has already received multiple customer requests.

“We are incredibly proud to welcome MTS ODYSSEE into our fleet” said Felix Vertommen, General Manager of Burando Energies ARA.

“This expansion reflects our ambition and our commitment to staying ahead of market developments. It enables us to directly respond to customer demand in Zeebrugge while supporting the transition toward biofuels and methanol.”

By expanding into Zeebrugge, the company said it has broadened its geographical reach and also strengthened its ability to respond to the evolving fuel mix required by shipowners and operators.

Photo credit: Burando Energies

Published: 6 March, 2026

Shandong Port has completed two ship-to-ship bunkering operations with a total of 2,500 metric tonnes (mt) of methanol for two international ships, according to Qingdao Maritime Safety Administration on Tuesday (3 March).

Bunkering vessel JIAN HANG LI DA delivered the methanol to COSCO SHIPPING GEMINI and MV CHANG MING at Qianwan port area of Qingdao Port at Shandong Port.

This marked the first time such operations were carried out using a model of one bunker vessel supplying multiple vessels.

It also signified that Shandong Port Group has officially established a complete green methanol supply chain from manufacturing to transportation and storage and then port bunkering, becoming the first port in Northern China to achieve a full-chain green methanol bunkering service for ships.

The green methanol used in this bunkering operation was produced within Shandong Province and is ISCC EU certified bio-methanol.

Photo credit: Qingdao Maritime Safety Administration

Published: 6 March, 2026

The transition towards digitalisation and automation is speeding up in the maritime industry, and a comprehensive strategy is emerging to turbo-charge efficiency, safety and sustainability.

Yet key questions remain about the adoption of technologies and solutions to enhance operational performance, future-proof fleets while remaining commercially competitive, and ultimately realise the zero-emission goal.

At Asia Pacific Maritime (APM) 2026, held from 25 to 27 March 2026 at Marina Bay Sands, Singapore, operational management will take centre stage as leading shipowners examine the strategies needed to navigate this transformation, powered by next energy and technological innovations.

APM 2026 will open with a welcome address from Guest-of-Honour Ang Wee Keong, Chief Executive of the Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA), followed by an opening keynote panel that will set the tone for the 3-day conference, bringing together 150 senior executives in maritime operations, technical directors, technology leaders and industry stakeholders.

With the overarching theme “Future of Vessel, Solutions for Tomorrow”, the APM 2026 conference will spotlight critical conversations shaping the future of maritime operations. Key discussion topics will include future fuels and zero-emission ships, smart and autonomous ships, next-gen shipbuilding, maritime cybersecurity, finance, risk and insurance for future fleet and electric and hybrid power.

The opening keynote panel, “The Maritime State of Play & What’s Next for Asia”, on 25 March, 11.10am to 12.00pm, will confront the core trade-offs facing industry leaders today: balancing immediate cost pressures with long-term decarbonisation goals, selecting viable fuel pathways, and strengthening resilience amid regulatory and trade flow uncertainty.

Moderated by Punit Oza, President of the Institute of Chartered Shipbrokers, the panel brings together:

Mohamed Safwan Othman, President and Group Managing Director, Dinastia Jati Group and Chairman, Malaysia Shipowners’ Association (MASA) and Federation of ASEAN Shipowners’ Association (FASA)

SK Lim, Managing Director, Pacific, G2 Ocean

Jotaro Tamura, Senior Managing Executive Officer, Mitsui OSK Lines

Joey Chua, Chair, Digitalisation Committee, Singapore Shipping Association

Ben Pike, COO, Swire Shipping

Roine Ahlquist, CCO, Wilhelmsen

“Shipowners across ASEAN are operating in an environment defined by tighter decarbonisation targets, rising compliance costs and increasing pressure to digitalise. The challenge is not a lack of ambition but the complexity of execution. Decisions around alternative fuels, fleet renewal and digital investments require careful calibration of capital, risk and long-term competitiveness,” said Mohamed Safwan Othman, President and Group Managing Director, Dinastia Jati Group and Chairman, Malaysia Shipowners’ Association (MASA) and Federation of ASEAN Shipowners’ Association (FASA).

“I look forward to productive discussions at APM with fellow C-Suite leavers on how to build resilience in this era of constant change.”

Beyond strategy, APM 2026 will address how transformation is unfolding at the vessel level. The panel discussion, “Securing LNG’s Long-Term Viability as a Shipping Fuel” on 26 March, 10.30am to 11.20am, will explore the renewed momentum behind LNG-fuelled vessels and assess whether LNG can remain competitive in a tightening decarbonisation landscape. While LNG remains the most established alternative fuel in shipping, increasing scrutiny around methane slip and lifecycle emissions has prompted deeper evaluation of its long-term role.

“LNG is no longer an experimental fuel; it is a proven solution with established infrastructure and operational experience. However, its long-term viability will depend on continuous technological evolution and broader industry adoption, particularly in reducing methane slip. The industry is also exploring the scalability of bio-LNG and e-LNG to maintain operational reliability and efficiency – a key topic we look forward to discussing at APM 2026,” said Sachin Saharawat, Technical Director, Eastern Pacific Shipping.

Note: More information on APM 2026 including the list of speakers, presentations and panel discussions can be found here.

Photo credit: RX Global

Published: 6 March, 2026

Pilbara Ports on Thursday (5 March) said it has taken a step further towards development of a low-carbon ammonia bunkering hub in the Pilbara, including safe and efficient ammonia handling, by signing a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Yara Pilbara.

The Yara Pilbara plant is one of the largest ammonia production facilities in the world, utilising world-class export infrastructure in the Port of Dampier.

The MoU advances the Pilbara Clean Fuel Bunkering Initiative through the two organisations agreeing to work collaboratively on government and stakeholder advocacy, developing a training program in safe ammonia handling, and in the planning of necessary fuelling infrastructure at the Ports of Dampier and Port of Port Hedand.

Pilbara Ports Chief Executive Officer Samuel McSkimming, said: “We’re pleased to work alongside Yara Pilbara to promote the production of clean marine fuels in the Pilbara, and on ensuring WA plays a leading role in the global transition to low-emission shipping,” Mr McSkimming said.

“The green iron corridor between the Pilbara and East Asia has the scale, stable demand, port infrastructure, and risk management experience to support the significant investment that maritime decarbonisation requires, while the Pilbara has the renewable energy resources, carbon capture and storage potential, and ammonia-based industry to lead in green fuel production.”

Currently, ships do not generally bunker in the Pilbara, instead taking on heavy fuel oil elsewhere for their entire outward-bound and return voyages.

Yara Pilbara Chief Operating Officer Laurent Trost said the company is committed to working with Pilbara Ports on decarbonising the shipping routes linking the Pilbara to established trading partners and emerging markets.

“Our operations represent Australia’s largest ammonia plant, we are an established producer in the region and part of a parent company that is a global leader in the application of low-carbon ammonia,” Mr Trost said.

“The vision of the Pilbara as a bunkering hub offers great promise and we are excited to be part of the push to make it happen.”

The Pilbara Ports and Yara Pilbara MOU marks another milestone in the Pilbara Clean Fuel Bunkering Hub strategic roadmap which involves significant industry collaboration.

Launched last year, the Pilbara Clean Fuel Bunkering Hub outlines a strategic approach to establish a clean fuel bunkering hub in the region, enabling the use of alternative fuels such as ammonia. One successful bunkering trial has already taken place and another is set to occur in the Port of Port Hedland later this year.

The first of its type in Australia, the bunkering hub would support decarbonisation efforts across the shipping, resource and maritime sectors, in line with the State Government’s 2050 emissions reduction target.

Pilbara Ports goal is for 100% of bulk export vessels departing the Pilbara to be powered by low carbon fuels, reinforcing its pivotal role in Australia’s iron ore trade and commitment to achieving net zero emissions by 2050.

Related: Pilbara Ports releases roadmap to establish Australia’s first clean fuel bunkering hub

Photo credit: Pilbara Ports

name: Shipping Industry
title: Hong Kong Completes First Green Methanol Bunkering, Driving Green Transformation of Its ...
description:
HONG KONG, March 6, 2026 - (ACN Newswire) – 5 March 2026, the world’s first methanol dual-fuel roll-on/roll-off vessel, CM HONG KONG, successfully completed its first green methanol bunkering operation in Hong Kong.
This also marks China's first green methanol bunkering operation conducted at anchor.
The achievement responds proactively to the “Dual Carbon” goals and advances implementation of Hong Kong’s Action Blueprint for Green Marine Fuel Bunkering, marking a solid step forward in Hong Kong’s development into an international green marine fuel bunkering centre.
A live screen broadcast the “first injection” of green methanol into CM HONG KONG, while simultaneously displaying the internationally certified green attribute certificate.
This inaugural bunkering is not merely a technical demonstration, but a pivotal practice in establishing Hong Kong’s green marine fuel bunkering ecosystem.
text:
HONG KONG, March 6, 2026 - (ACN Newswire) – 5 March 2026, the world’s first methanol dual-fuel roll-on/roll-off vessel, CM HONG KONG, successfully completed its first green methanol bunkering operation in Hong Kong. This also marks China's first green methanol bunkering operation conducted at anchor.

This milestone signifies a major breakthrough in the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region Government’s deepened collaboration with enterprises including China Merchants Group (CMG), China Petroleum & Chemical Corporation (Sinopec Group), and China International Marine Containers Group (CIMC). The achievement responds proactively to the “Dual Carbon” goals and advances implementation of Hong Kong’s Action Blueprint for Green Marine Fuel Bunkering, marking a solid step forward in Hong Kong’s development into an international green marine fuel bunkering centre.

The bunkering ceremony, themed “RIDING THE TIDE, GREENING THE FUTURE”, was jointly hosted by China Merchants Energy Shipping (CMES), Sinopec (Hong Kong), and CIMC Enric at the Legislative Council Complex. Officiating guests included the Secretary for Transport and Logistics of the Government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, the Hon Mable CHAN, JP; the Under Secretary for Environment and Ecology, Diane WONG Shuk-han, JP; the Director of Marine, WONG Sai-fat, together with Members of the Legislative Council, the Hon Gary CHAN Hak-kan, SBS, JP; the Hon Judy CHAN Kapui, MH, JP; the Hon Erik YIM Kong, JP; the Hon Aaron BOK Kwok-ming; Dr the Hon Webster NG Kam-wah, JP; Capt the Hon Lothair LAM Ming-fung; and the Hon Tommy CHUNG Ki-fung. Representatives from CMG, Sinopec, and CIMC, along with more than 100 distinguished guests from the shipping, energy, equipment manufacturing, and financial sectors, witnessed the significant moment.

During the ceremony, officiating guests jointly launched the bunkering operation. A live screen broadcast the “first injection” of green methanol into CM HONG KONG, while simultaneously displaying the internationally certified green attribute certificate.

The vessel completed bunkering via a ship-to-ship (STS) transfer, receiving approximately 500 tonnes of green methanol. According to calculations, this volume is expected to reduce greenhouse gas emissions by approximately 85% compared with conventional marine fuels.

The green methanol was produced and supplied by CIMC Enric and has obtained ISCC EU certification. The bunkering operation was carried out through Sinopec (Hong Kong)’s professional bunkering service system, and the fuel was ultimately applied by CMES as the shipowner. The operation fully demonstrated a closed-loop green methanol value chain encompassing “production–storage–shipping–bunkering–application”.

Pursuant to the Strategic Cooperation Agreement on Jointly Building the Hong Kong Green Methanol Bunkering Centre signed in November 2025, the three parties leveraged their respective core strengths in ocean shipping, oil and gas and green fuel storage-transport-sales, and energy equipment manufacturing. Technical validation, solution optimisation, and emergency preparedness were completed in advance to ensure smooth implementation.

To ensure safe and efficient operations, relevant HKSAR Government departments worked in close coordination with participating enterprises. The Transport and Logistics Bureau, Marine Department, Customs and Excise Department, and Immigration Department provided end-to-end support in policy guidance, streamlined approvals, and safety oversight in accordance with the Action Plan on Green Maritime Fuel Bunkering.

This inaugural bunkering is not merely a technical demonstration, but a pivotal practice in establishing Hong Kong’s green marine fuel bunkering ecosystem. It verifies both the technical feasibility and commercial operability of green methanol bunkering in Hong Kong, and showcases a “Hong Kong’s Approach” characterised by government-enterprise collaboration, port-shipping synergy, and full industry-chain coordination. This initiative helps attract more green vessels to call at Hong Kong and encourages Hong Kong-registered vessels to increase their usage of green marine fuels, striving to achieve the target of 7% by 2030.

The three organizations that collaborated to complete this bunkering operation stated that they would continue to further deepen cooperation, promote the industrialisation, scaling, and regularisation of green methanol supply in Hong Kong, and accelerate the establishment of a comprehensive ecosystem covering production, storage, shipping, bunkering, trading, and financial services. These efforts will support Hong Kong in building a leading green maritime fuel bunkering and trading centre in the Asia-Pacific region, contributing to the national “Dual Carbon” goals, Hong Kong’s carbon neutrality, and the global low-carbon transformation of the shipping industry.

name: International Maritime Organization
title: Industry coalition urges IMO member states to adopt net-zero framework in 2026
description:
The signatories said the framework represents a balanced compromise capable of providing a credible pathway toward net zero emissions from international shipping by or around 2050.
Shipping was characterised as the backbone of international trade, with global regulation creating a shared system covering emissions from international shipping.
Early signals from the IMO about regulating shipping emissions have already triggered the production of low-carbon fuels and technologies in multiple regions while efficiency measures are being introduced across the industry.
The coalition said adopting the framework would help create markets for new vessels and technologies and support economic activity linked to the sector’s transition.
It warned that uncertainty created by delaying adoption could weaken international investment in solutions required for shipping’s energy transition and risk reversing progress already underway.
text:
A coalition of shipping, logistics, energy and technology companies has urged governments to adopt the International Maritime Organization’s Net-Zero Framework in 2026, warning that delays could undermine investment in fuels, vessels and infrastructure needed for the sector’s energy transition, according to an industry call to action.

The signatories said the framework represents a balanced compromise capable of providing a credible pathway toward net zero emissions from international shipping by or around 2050.

Shipping was characterised as the backbone of international trade, with global regulation creating a shared system covering emissions from international shipping.

A single framework would establish one set of rules for the sector and provide clarity for companies investing in new fuels and technologies.

Early signals from the IMO about regulating shipping emissions have already triggered the production of low-carbon fuels and technologies in multiple regions while efficiency measures are being introduced across the industry.

According to the statement, vessels capable of operating on these fuels are already being ordered and delivered, and bunkering infrastructure is being developed globally.

The coalition said adopting the framework would help create markets for new vessels and technologies and support economic activity linked to the sector’s transition.

It warned that uncertainty created by delaying adoption could weaken international investment in solutions required for shipping’s energy transition and risk reversing progress already underway.

The statement was signed by dozens of maritime, logistics, energy and technology companies including Alfa Laval, CMB Tech, DFDS, J. Lauritzen, Klaveness Combination Carriers, Kuehne+Nagel, Pacific Basin Shipping, X-Press Feeders, Yara International and engine developer WinGD.

title: EU Adopts New Maritime and Ports Strategies for Leadership and Decarbonisation
description:
EU Adopts New Maritime and Ports Strategies for Leadership and Decarbonisation The European Commission has adopted a pair of strategies aimed at the bloc's waterborne sector, according to Splash247.
The EU Industrial Maritime Strategy is designed to strengthen Europe's maritime leadership through actions that include launching an alliance for maritime value chains.
The separate EU Ports Strategy will promote innovation, digitalisation and integration with other transport infrastructure.
It will develop guidance on foreign ownership of EU ports and for EU funding and investments in ports outside the bloc.
Lucy Gilliam, co-executive director at the NGO One Planet Port, commented on the potential for European ports to become global leaders in climate innovation and ecosystem stewardship.
text:
EU Adopts New Maritime and Ports Strategies for Leadership and Decarbonisation

The European Commission has adopted a pair of strategies aimed at the bloc's waterborne sector, according to Splash247. The EU Industrial Maritime Strategy is designed to strengthen Europe's maritime leadership through actions that include launching an alliance for maritime value chains. This initiative is intended to advance high-tech shipbuilding, offshore wind support vessels, underwater drones, and port equipment.

A research and innovation flagship call will support testing innovative solutions in real-world shipyard environments, with a goal of scaling successful technologies. To foster competitive shipping, the commission will establish a dialogue with member states to promote EU flags and streamline administrative formalities related to certain maritime regulations.

The separate EU Ports Strategy will promote innovation, digitalisation and integration with other transport infrastructure. It will develop guidance on foreign ownership of EU ports and for EU funding and investments in ports outside the bloc. To advance a clean energy transition for ports, the strategy presents measures promoting electrification and improved grid connections.

Sotiris Raptis, secretary-general of the European shipowners' association, stated that the commission had rejected protectionist calls and prioritized investment. He argued this approach protects competitiveness while delivering decarbonisation and strengthening energy security. Raptis further urged that EU green measures should be transitional and be withdrawn if a global agreement is adopted at the International Maritime Organization.

Lucy Gilliam, co-executive director at the NGO One Planet Port, commented on the potential for European ports to become global leaders in climate innovation and ecosystem stewardship. She noted that integrating marine spatial planning, nature-based design, circular production systems with zero emission and wind-assisted shipping into a coherent strategy could build ports that are resilient, competitive and aligned with planetary boundaries.

name: Maritime Transport
title: EU launches Industrial Maritime and Ports stategies - SAFETY4SEA
description:
On 4 March, the European Commission adopted a EU Industrial Maritime Strategy and a Ports Strategy to drive competitiveness, sustainability, decarbonization, security and resilience within the EU’s wider waterborne sector.
Strengthening Europe’s maritime leadership The EU’s new maritime strategies signal a clear ambition to consolidate Europe’s leadership in global maritime industries.
With our EU Ports and Industrial Maritime Strategies, we are equipping Europe’s ports, shipping and shipbuilding sectors to lead the clean energy transition, secure trade and defence, and remain globally competitive.
They renew our ambition for European maritime leadership, reinforcing economic security, driving sustainable growth, and supporting quality jobs and territorial cohesion across Europe.
Recent events have again reminded us that European maritime and trade resilience extend far beyond its coastline.
text:
On 4 March, the European Commission adopted a EU Industrial Maritime Strategy and a Ports Strategy to drive competitiveness, sustainability, decarbonization, security and resilience within the EU’s wider waterborne sector.

Strengthening Europe’s maritime leadership

The EU’s new maritime strategies signal a clear ambition to consolidate Europe’s leadership in global maritime industries. Shipbuilding, shipping, and port equipment are treated as strategic sectors that support economic resilience, technological sovereignty, and defence preparedness. The EU’s reasoning is that strengthening these industries internally will reduce strategic dependencies and secure long-term competitiveness.

With our EU Ports and Industrial Maritime Strategies, we are equipping Europe’s ports, shipping and shipbuilding sectors to lead the clean energy transition, secure trade and defence, and remain globally competitive. They renew our ambition for European maritime leadership, reinforcing economic security, driving sustainable growth, and supporting quality jobs and territorial cohesion across Europe.

… highlighted Commissioner for Sustainable Transport and Tourism, Apostolos Tzitzikostas.

Boosting competitiveness and strategic autonomy

The strategy seeks to make EU shipping more competitive while maintaining climate ambition. The Commission plans to reduce administrative burdens and strengthen the attractiveness of EU flags, ensuring that regulatory frameworks support rather than hinder growth.

Scope includes:

Promoting EU flags and simplifying administrative formalities

Streamlining monitoring and reporting under EU ETS Maritime and FuelEU Maritime

Leveraging public procurement to stimulate innovation and decarbonization

Strengthening export financing and trade tools to ensure fair global competition

Expanding naval production capacity and supporting dual-use ferry construction

By combining economic, regulatory, and defence measures, the EU aims to protect its industrial base while reinforcing strategic autonomy.

The Industrial Maritime Strategies reflect our new method, based on a strategic dialogue and delivering a business-plan approach covering all strategic pillars for the value chain : from decarbonization to digitalization challenges, from boosting demand to ensuring a level playing field and integrating dual-use specificities, with simplification measures and new dedicated financing

… commented Executive Vice-President for Prosperity and Industrial Strategy, Stéphane Séjourné.

Transforming ports into strategic hubs

Ports are viewed as essential infrastructure, handling around three quarters of the EU’s external trade and playing a central role in energy supply, defence, and the blue economy. The EU wants to accelerate their transformation into innovation-driven and clean energy hubs.

Scope includes:

Promoting digitalization and integration with wider transport networks

Supporting electrification and improved grid connections

Developing guidance on foreign ownership of EU ports

Providing direction for EU funding and investments in third-country ports

Creating a roadmap for competitive small and medium-sized ports

These actions reflect the EU’s intention to maintain control over strategic assets while boosting the competitiveness and sustainability of its port system.

Enhancing security and resilience

Given their role as key entry and exit points, ports are also seen as vulnerable to organized crime and external threats. The EU aims to strengthen supply chain security and reduce exposure to emerging risks.

Scope includes:

Reinforcing maritime security legislation

Introducing frameworks for port worker background checks

Assessing risks linked to third-country ports

Conducting an EU-wide cybersecurity risk assessment

Establishing a forum for cooperation among cybersecurity and port authorities

In addition, the creation of a high-level Maritime Industries and Ports Board underlines the EU’s determination to coordinate these efforts at the political level.

Industry response

The industry has broadly welcomed the Commission’s strategies, seeing them as a strong signal that maritime industries are central to Europe’s competitiveness, security, and resilience. At the same time, they have used the opportunity to underline the importance of global alignment, investment certainty, and social safeguards.

Danish Shipping welcomed the Commission’s focus on strengthening Europe’s maritime cluster. Anna Vejlby Ib, Head of Danish Shipping’s EU Representation, stated: “It is positive that the Commission recognizes the strategic importance of European shipping for Europe and focuses on developing and strengthening the European maritime cluster so that we can continue to compete globally in the future.”

European Shipowners | ECSA also welcomed the Industrial Maritime Strategy. Reflecting on geopolitical instability, the association noted that the Strategy recognizes the strategic role of shipping for Europe’s energy and supply chain security and proposes measures to strengthen international competitiveness.

Although the European fleet is growing steadily, other fleets are growing faster. The Commission has rejected any protectionist calls and instead has prioritized investment. This is how we protect competitiveness, deliver decarbonization and strengthen Europe’s energy security at the same time

… said European Shipowners | ECSA Secretary General, Sotiris Raptis.

The World Shipping Council also welcomed the Commission’s focus on port security and maritime decarbonization. On security, it supported stronger public private partnerships and improved information sharing.

Recent events have again reminded us that European maritime and trade resilience extend far beyond its coastline. The strategies rightly recognize the importance of ensuring safe passage along key maritime routes to keep trade moving

… commented WSC President and CEO, Joe Kramek.

In addition, Melina Travlos is the President of the Union of Greek Shipowners stated: “The Strategy comes at a critical moment for the EU,” highlighting that “shipping must remain at the core of Europe’s strategy. Its competitiveness underpins the resilience and long-term prosperity of Europe.”

Trade union perspective and social dimension

The European Transport Workers’ Federation (ETF) welcomed the EU Ports Strategy as “a timely initiative at a moment of geopolitical uncertainty, supply-chain pressures, and climate transformation.” Berardina Tommasi, ETF Senior Policy Officer for Dockers, highlighted the strategy’s recognition of workers, health and safety focus, and commitment to social dialogue, while noting concerns about increasing competition through shorter port concessions:

Ports are not only infrastructure assets — they are major workplaces employing thousands of skilled workers who keep operations safe, resilient, and efficient every day. A truly strategic port policy balances competitiveness with quality jobs, strong labor standards, and meaningful social dialogue.

… highlighted Berardina Tommasi.

In addition, Nikolaos Koletsis, ETF Senior Policy Officer for Maritime Transport, praised the EU’s holistic approach linking shipbuilding and maritime transport but called for stronger measures relating to working conditions:

There is a proven link between working conditions and safety – safety at sea depends on people, and Europe’s resilience requires dual-use ferries that are built, flagged and crewed in Europe. International standards must also be reviewed to address excessive working hours that still affect seafarers.”

title: Sulnox, Pan Marine Partner to Distribute Eco Fuel in Egypt
description:
Sulnox Group Plc, a UK-based greentech company, has signed a distribution agreement with Pan Marine Petroleum Services (Pan Marine).
Pan Marine, a leading integrated maritime and energy services provider in Egypt with strong nationwide distribution capabilities, serves nearly 1,500 clients across more than 20 locations nationwide.
“Working with Pan Marine materially strengthens Sulnox’s position in the Suez Canal region,” Ben Richardson, CEO of Sulnox, commented.
Pan Marine has moved to adopt Sulnox Eco across its own fleet, which includes platform support vessels, a container ship, and a RoPax.
Pan Marine operates out of strategically positioned Free Zone hubs in Al-Amerya and Suez.
text:
Sulnox Group Plc, a UK-based greentech company, has signed a distribution agreement with Pan Marine Petroleum Services (Pan Marine). The collaboration integrates Sulnox’s fuel-saving and emission-reducing technology with Pan Marine’s nationwide distribution network, according to a press release on the agreement.

Pan Marine, a leading integrated maritime and energy services provider in Egypt with strong nationwide distribution capabilities, serves nearly 1,500 clients across more than 20 locations nationwide. The company will market Sulnox Eco, a 100% organic, biodegradable fuel conditioner, through its established platforms, which have previously scaled major brands such as Castrol, a global producer of premium automotive and industrial lubricants.

Sulnox Eco is designed to improve combustion efficiency and storage stability for both traditional fuels and biofuel blends. This is increasingly relevant as shipping companies adopt biofuels to meet tougher European Union (EU) emissions rules for journeys between Asia and Europe, noted the press release.

“Working with Pan Marine materially strengthens Sulnox’s position in the Suez Canal region,” Ben Richardson, CEO of Sulnox, commented. Richardson noted that intensifying regulatory pressure is driving operators toward solutions that maintain fuel stability and performance.

The partnership targets the Suez Canal, a vital artery carrying approximately 12% of global seaborne trade and up to 30% of global container traffic. The canal facilitates more than 20,000 vessel transits annually, creating significant demand for fuel efficiency and emissions management

As part of the agreement, Pan Marine has already placed its first commercial order.

Pan Marine has moved to adopt Sulnox Eco across its own fleet, which includes platform support vessels, a container ship, and a RoPax. The company bypassed traditional trials after reviewing independent data and case studies from maritime operators like Eastern Pacific Shipping, according to the release.

“Operators need solutions that deliver measurable fuel efficiency and emissions improvements without operational disruption,” Marwan El Shazly, Vice President of Marketing Contracts at Pan Marine, said. He added that the product is a natural extension of Pan Marine’s portfolio as global fuel standards evolve.

Pan Marine operates out of strategically positioned Free Zone hubs in Al-Amerya and Suez. These locations anchor operations at both the Mediterranean and Red Sea gateways, enabling efficient distribution across one of the world’s most critical maritime corridors.
